title: Mathematics
subtitle: Week 2: Metric Area and Geometry Basics

slides.title: Metric and Geometry Slide 1
slides.text: We measure the area of small shapes using square centimeters (sq. cm). A square centimeter is the space covered by a small square with sides that are exactly one centimeter long. For larger areas like floors and fields, we use square meters (sq. m), where each side is one meter long. In Koronadal City, students measure their small math notebooks in square centimeters, while farmers in Tupi measure their fruit vegetable beds in square meters. When should we choose square centimeters instead of square meters to measure a surface? Let us think about objects. Card 1. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to.

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Area of a table = 3m multiplied by 2m = 6 sq. m. A point is labeled as Point A.

slides.title: Metric and Geometry Slide 6
slides.text: Solving area word problems helps us calculate actual spaces. When solving a problem, identify the shape first, write down the given side lengths, and use the correct metric unit in your final answer. Remember that area answers must always be written in square units like sq. cm or sq. m. A market vendor in General Santos City wants to find the area of a rectangular tuna fish stall that is 4 meters long and 3 meters wide. How does writing our final answer with the correct unit help others understand our measurements? Study detail 6. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice.

slides.title: Metric and Geometry Slide 11
slides.text: In geometry, a point is an exact location in space. It has no size, length, or width, and is represented by a small dot. We name points using capital letters. A point helps us mark specific positions on grids, shapes, and maps. On a regional map of South Cotabato, a small red dot represents the exact location of the municipal hall of Polomolok town. Why do we use points to mark positions on a map, and how do capital letters help us identify them? Slide 11. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Area of a table = 3m multiplied by 2m = 6 sq. m. A point is labeled as Point A.

slides.title: Metric and Geometry Slide 16
slides.text: A line is a straight path of points that goes on forever in both directions. In drawing, we show this by putting arrowheads on both ends of the path. A line has no endpoints, meaning it has infinite length and cannot be measured with a ruler. A long straight highway segment between General Santos City and Koronadal City represents a geometric line extending into the distance. How do the arrowheads on a drawn line tell us that it continues without stopping in both directions? Card 16. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

slides.title: Metric and Geometry Slide 21
slides.text: A line segment is a part of a line that has two fixed endpoints, meaning it has a definite length that we can measure. A ray is another part of a line, but it has only one endpoint and extends forever in the other direction, represented by one arrowhead. A wooden ruler used by weavers in Lake Sebu is a line segment, while a beam of sunlight shining on Mount Matutum is a ray. What is the difference between a line segment that we can measure and a ray that goes on forever? Slide 21. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice.

challenge.q: A rectangular vegetable garden in Tupi has an area of 30 sq. m. If the length is 5 meters, what is the width of the garden?
A rectangular vegetable garden in Tupi has an area of 40 sq. m. If the length is 6 meters, what is the width of the garden?
A rectangular vegetable garden in Tupi has an area of 50 sq. m. If the length is 7 meters, what is the width of the garden?
A rectangular vegetable garden in Tupi has an area of 60 sq. m. If the length is 8 meters, what is the width of the garden?
A rectangular vegetable garden in Tupi has an area of 70 sq. m. If the length is 9 meters, what is the width of the garden?

pages:
Exercise 1: Metric Area Problems

Calculate the area using the formula and write your answer with the correct unit (sq. cm or sq. m).

1. A rectangular tuna packing box in GenSan is 80 cm long and 50 cm wide. What is its area?
4000 sq. cm

Exercise 2: Identifying Geometry Figures

Look at the drawings and write whether it is a Point, Line, Line Segment, or Ray.

1. A straight path with arrowheads on both ends:
Line
2. A straight path with two solid endpoints:
Line Segment

Exercise 3: Geometry Applications

Write the name of the real-world model represented by each geometric concept.

1. A beam of light coming from a flashlight:
Ray

performance.title: Geometry and Area Map of a Park
performance.desc: Design a small park layout in Koronadal City on paper. Draw a rectangular playground that is 10 cm long and 8 cm wide, and calculate its area. Near the playground, draw a straight walking path (representing a line segment), a streetlamp shining light downwards (representing a ray), and a fountain marker (representing a point). Label all figures clearly, take a photo, and upload it.
labels: Correct labels for Point, Segment, and Ray
